original_reasoning: Arabic guarding/enclosing a field or flock overlaps well with Greek 'you had/held'; both preserve a possession-containment core.
Arabic passing heavily carries a clear bulk or weight notion, which aligns well with Greek large in size.
خزق denotes piercing or splitting with an implement, and σχίζα is a cut-off piece; the Greek noun can be the result of the Arabic action.
To approve, admire, or find good can readily drift into rejoicing or exultation.
Arabic bribery works by causing someone to let pass or release restraint; Greek release/loosening fits that functional conceptual core well.
حرق centers on burning/scorching, and the plant name can plausibly preserve a 'burning, caustic' property of wood spurge.
Repairing or setting right an opinion aligns well with restored order and rhythm.
Arabic jumuḥ is bolting, unruly going out, and Greek is rousing battle; both share forceful breakout into conflict.
